summary: This is a brief announcement post about a talk Julia Evans gave at Localhost, the Recurse Center's monthly talk series. The talk covers the technical core of rbspy, her Ruby profiler tool, focusing on how it reads memory from the Ruby interpreter to determine what function a process is running. She highlights the challenges of supporting multiple Ruby versions and whether the Ruby process needs to be stopped during profiling. The post is essentially a pointer to the 30-minute video talk followed by 30 minutes of Q&A.
key_points: rbspy works by reading memory directly out of the Ruby interpreter to determine what function is currently running
Supporting multiple Ruby versions is a key technical challenge for the profiler
The profiler aims to work without stopping the Ruby process being profiled
The Recurse Center's Localhost format encourages deep technical talks with extensive audience Q&A
money_quotes: How do we read memory out of the Ruby interpreter to figure out what function a Ruby process is running?
My favourite thing about Localhost's talk format is that speakers give relatively in depth talks about technical topics, and then people ask lots of questions at the end.
key_insight: Building a Ruby profiler requires reading memory directly from the Ruby interpreter across multiple versions without stopping the target process.
